expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the action potential—the fundamental electrical signal of excitable cells (neurons, muscle fibers)—detailing the ionic basis of the resting membrane potential, the voltage-gated ion channels that generate depolarization and repolarization, the refractory periods that ensure unidirectional propagation, and the mechanisms of saltatory conduction in myelinated axons. Emphasis is placed on the biophysical principles (Nernst equation, Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation, Hodgkin-Huxley model), the structural basis of channel function (voltage-sensing domains, inactivation gates), and clinical correlations (channelopathies, local anesthetics, demyelinating diseases).

core_concept.principle: The action potential is a rapid, all-or-none depolarization of the neuronal membrane, initiated when threshold is reached, driven by voltage-gated sodium channels (rapid Na+ influx causing depolarization) and terminated by voltage-gated potassium channels (K+ efflux causing repolarization)—with propagation in myelinated axons occurring via saltatory conduction, where action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier, dramatically increasing conduction velocity and energy efficiency.
core_concept.explanation:
The action potential is the fundamental mechanism by which neurons encode and transmit information over long distances. It arises from the coordinated activity of voltage-gated ion channels embedded in the axonal membrane.

The resting membrane potential (approximately -65 to -70 mV in neurons) is established by the sodium-potassium pump (Na+/K+-ATPase, maintaining high intracellular K+ and low intracellular Na+), potassium leak channels (making the membrane selectively permeable to K+ at rest), and the Donnan effect of impermeant intracellular anions. The Nernst equation predicts the equilibrium potential for each ion (EK+ ≈ -90 mV, ENa+ ≈ +60 mV); the resting potential lies between these values, closest to EK+ due to high resting K+ permeability.

Depolarization occurs when voltage-gated sodium channels (Nav) open in response to membrane depolarization. These channels have a voltage-sensing domain (S4 segment with positively charged residues) that moves outward upon depolarization, triggering channel opening. Na+ influx down its electrochemical gradient rapidly drives the membrane toward ENa+ (overshoot to +30 to +50 mV).

Repolarization results from two processes: (1) inactivation of sodium channels (the intracellular inactivation gate closes within milliseconds, blocking Na+ influx despite continued depolarization); (2) delayed opening of voltage-gated potassium channels (Kv), which allow K+ efflux, restoring the negative resting potential.

Hyperpolarization (afterhyperpolarization, AHP) occurs because Kv channels close slowly, causing membrane potential to transiently dip below resting level.

The refractory period—absolute (no second action potential possible due to sodium channel inactivation) and relative (second action potential possible only with stronger stimulus due to residual K+ channel opening)—ensures unidirectional propagation and sets the maximum firing frequency.

Saltatory conduction in myelinated axons: myelin (produced by Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) increases membrane resistance and decreases capacitance, preventing depolarization along internodes. Voltage-gated sodium channels are concentrated at nodes of Ranvier; action potentials regenerate only at nodes, with local currents flowing electrotonically through internodes to depolarize the next node. This increases conduction velocity (up to 120 m/s in large myelinated axons vs. 0.5-2 m/s in unmyelinated) while reducing energy consumption (Na+/K+-ATPase activity reduced ~100-fold).

Understanding action potential generation and propagation is essential for clinical disciplines: neurology (channelopathies—epilepsy, periodic paralysis, cardiac arrhythmias), anesthesiology (local anesthetics block Nav channels), neurology (demyelinating diseases—multiple sclerosis, Guillain-Barré syndrome), and cardiology (antiarrhythmic drugs target cardiac ion channels).

image.alt: Graph of action potential showing phases: resting potential, threshold, depolarization (Na+ influx), repolarization (K+ efflux, Na+ inactivation), afterhyperpolarization. Below: corresponding sodium and potassium channel states (closed, open, inactivated).
image.caption: Action Potential Phases: Resting potential (~-70 mV) → Threshold (~-55 mV) → Depolarization (Na+ influx, overshoot to +30 mV) → Repolarization (Na+ inactivation, K+ efflux) → Afterhyperpolarization (delayed K+ closure). Voltage-gated sodium and potassium channels gate the ionic currents.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Ionic Basis of Electrical Signaling
sections.introduction:
The action potential is the currency of neural communication—a brief, all-or-none electrical impulse that travels along axons to convey information over distances ranging from micrometers to meters. Understanding its generation requires mastery of the ionic environment of neurons, the properties of ion channels, and the biophysical principles that govern membrane potential.

At rest, neurons maintain a stable membrane potential (resting potential) through the coordinated actions of ion pumps (Na+/K+-ATPase) and leak channels (primarily K+). This resting potential stores electrochemical potential energy that can be released upon stimulation. When a stimulus depolarizes the membrane to threshold, voltage-gated sodium channels undergo a rapid conformational change, opening to allow Na+ influx. The resulting positive feedback (depolarization opens more sodium channels) drives the membrane toward the sodium equilibrium potential, generating the upstroke of the action potential.

Within milliseconds, sodium channels inactivate, and voltage-gated potassium channels open, repolarizing the membrane. The refractory periods that follow ensure that action potentials travel in one direction and limit firing frequency.

In myelinated axons, this process is optimized: myelin sheaths insulate the axonal membrane, restricting depolarization to the nodes of Ranvier, where sodium channels are concentrated. Action potentials jump from node to node—saltatory conduction—dramatically increasing speed and efficiency.

The ionic basis of the action potential was first elucidated by Hodgkin and Huxley in the squid giant axon (1952), a discovery that earned them the Nobel Prize and established the foundation for modern neurobiology. Today, this knowledge underpins clinical understanding of channelopathies (genetic disorders of ion channels), the mechanism of local anesthetics, and the pathophysiology of demyelinating diseases.

introduction_image.alt: Diagram of a neuron with axon; zoomed view of axonal membrane showing ion channels: Na+/K+ pump, K+ leak channels, voltage-gated Na+ channels, voltage-gated K+ channels. Icons show ion gradients (high Na+ outside, high K+ inside).
introduction_image.caption: Ionic Basis of the Action Potential: The Na+/K+ pump maintains ion gradients (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in). Resting permeability to K+ (leak channels) sets resting potential near EK+. Depolarization activates voltage-gated Na+ channels (Na+ influx). Repolarization involves Na+ channel inactivation and voltage-gated K+ channel activation.

key_concept.definition: The action potential is an all-or-none, regenerative depolarization driven by voltage-gated sodium channels, terminated by sodium channel inactivation and voltage-gated potassium channel activation, with propagation occurring via local currents—saltatory in myelinated axons.
key_concept.context:
The all-or-none property distinguishes action potentials from graded potentials (EPSPs, IPSPs). Once threshold is reached, the action potential amplitude is invariant; information is encoded in frequency, not amplitude. The regenerative nature arises from positive feedback: depolarization opens Na+ channels → Na+ influx → further depolarization. This ensures rapid, reliable propagation.

Propagation occurs via local currents: depolarization at one region spreads electrostatically to adjacent regions, depolarizing them to threshold. In unmyelinated axons, this occurs continuously; in myelinated axons, local currents travel through internodes to the next node (saltatory conduction).

Clinical relevance: Local anesthetics (lidocaine, bupivacaine) block voltage-gated sodium channels, preventing action potential initiation and propagation—the basis for regional anesthesia. Mutations in Nav channels cause channelopathies: epilepsy (SCN1A, SCN2A), periodic paralysis (SCN4A), cardiac arrhythmias (SCN5A—long QT syndrome). Demyelination (multiple sclerosis) slows or blocks saltatory conduction, causing neurological deficits.

image.alt: Graphs showing subthreshold stimuli (graded depolarization, no action potential) vs. suprathreshold stimuli (all-or-none action potential). Diagram showing local current propagation along axon.
image.caption: All-or-None Principle: Subthreshold stimuli produce only local graded potentials. Suprathreshold stimuli trigger a full action potential. Local currents from the active region depolarize adjacent membrane, propagating the signal without decrement.

sections.key_concept: The action potential is generated by sequential opening and closing of voltage-gated ion channels; the resting membrane potential is established by ion gradients and selective permeability; saltatory conduction in myelinated axons optimizes speed and efficiency.

classifications.type: Ionic Basis: Resting Potential, Depolarization, Repolarization
classifications.value: Best for understanding the biophysics of membrane potential, the role of individual ion species, and the gating mechanisms of voltage-gated channels.
characteristics: Resting Membrane Potential: Typically -65 to -70 mV in neurons. Determined by: (1) Na+/K+-ATPase (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in per ATP, maintains gradients); (2) K+ leak channels (K2P family)—high resting K+ permeability; (3) Nernst potentials: EK = (RT/zF) ln([K+]out/[K+]in) ≈ -90 mV; ENa ≈ +60 mV; (4) Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation accounts for multiple permeant ions: Vm = 58 log10((PK[K+]out + PNa[Na+]out + PCl[Cl-]in)/(PK[K+]in + PNa[Na+]in + PCl[Cl-]out)). At rest, PK dominates, so Vm near EK.

Depolarization (Upstroke): Triggered by voltage-gated sodium channels (Nav1.1, Nav1.2, Nav1.6 in CNS; Nav1.4 in muscle; Nav1.5 in heart). Channel structure: α subunit (24 transmembrane segments, 4 homologous domains, each with 6 segments); S4 segment contains positively charged lysine/arginine residues (voltage sensor). Depolarization causes S4 outward movement → channel opens → Na+ influx (down electrochemical gradient) → membrane potential approaches ENa (+30 to +50 mV). Positive feedback: Na+ influx → depolarization → more Na+ channels open. Peak Na+ conductance: ~100 mS/cm².

Repolarization: (1) Sodium channel inactivation—intracellular inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) swings into channel pore within 1-2 ms, blocking Na+ influx even when channel is depolarized. (2) Delayed rectifier potassium channels (Kv1, Kv7, Kv11 families) open with delay (~1-2 ms after depolarization), allowing K+ efflux, restoring negative potential. (3) Afterhyperpolarization (AHP) results from slow closure of K+ channels and activation of calcium-activated K+ channels (SK, BK) in some neurons.

Refractory Periods: Absolute refractory period—Na+ channels inactivated, cannot reopen regardless of stimulus strength; ensures unidirectional propagation, sets maximum firing rate (typically 100-1000 Hz). Relative refractory period—some Na+ channels recovered, but K+ channels still open, making threshold higher; allows frequency encoding.
classifications.behavior:
The action potential is a dynamic interplay between sodium and potassium conductances. Hodgkin-Huxley model describes this mathematically: I = Cm (dV/dt) + gNa m³h (V - ENa) + gK n⁴ (V - EK) + gL (V - EL). Variables m (Na activation, fast), h (Na inactivation, slower), n (K activation, slower) are voltage-dependent gating variables. Key dynamics: At rest, m ≈ 0, h ≈ 1, n ≈ 0. Depolarization increases m rapidly (milliseconds), opening Na channels; h decreases more slowly, causing inactivation. n increases slowly, opening K channels. The time constants and voltage dependencies produce the characteristic action potential waveform. Temperature dependence: Q10 of 2-3 for ion channel kinetics; cooling slows conduction.

Clinical correlations: Hyperkalemia (elevated [K+]out) depolarizes resting potential (shifts toward EK), inactivating Na channels → muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias; hypokalemia hyperpolarizes, increases excitability → arrhythmias. Local anesthetics bind to Nav channels in inactivated state, stabilizing inactivation and preventing firing. Tetrodotoxin (TTX, pufferfish) and saxitoxin (paralytic shellfish poisoning) block Nav channels (TTX-sensitive channels: Nav1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.6, 1.7; TTX-resistant: Nav1.5, 1.8, 1.9).

examples.process: Hodgkin-Huxley Model and Voltage-Gated Channel Kinetics
examples.explanation:
The Hodgkin-Huxley model quantitatively describes action potential generation: (1) Voltage clamp experiments revealed distinct Na+ and K+ currents. (2) Na+ current activates rapidly (τ ~0.1-0.5 ms at depolarized potentials) and inactivates with slower kinetics (τ ~1-2 ms). (3) K+ current activates with delay (τ ~1-5 ms) and does not inactivate significantly during the action potential. (4) Gating variables: m³ (Na activation, cooperative, 3 independent gates), h (Na inactivation, single gate), n⁴ (K activation, cooperative, 4 independent gates). (5) Mathematical modeling reproduces action potential waveform, refractory periods, and frequency response.

Significance: The Hodgkin-Huxley model is the foundation of computational neuroscience. Understanding gating kinetics explains: (1) Why action potentials have a threshold (cooperative activation creates positive feedback); (2) Why there is an absolute refractory period (h variable must recover from inactivation); (3) Why maximum firing rate is limited by channel kinetics.

Clinical application: Antiarrhythmic drugs (Class I) block cardiac Nav channels with state-dependent kinetics (favoring open or inactivated states), reducing excitability in arrhythmogenic tissue.

image.alt: Graphs showing voltage-clamp recordings of Na+ and K+ currents; gating variables m, h, n over time; circuit diagram of Hodgkin-Huxley model with variable conductances.
image.caption: Hodgkin-Huxley Model: Voltage-gated Na+ current (rapid activation/inactivation) and K+ current (delayed activation) generate the action potential. Gating variables m (Na activation), h (Na inactivation), and n (K activation) have distinct voltage-dependent kinetics.

examples.explanation:
Refractory periods shape neural coding: (1) Absolute refractory period (ARP): Na+ channels inactivated; no stimulus can elicit another action potential. Duration ~1-2 ms in mammalian neurons, limiting maximum firing frequency to ~500-1000 Hz. (2) Relative refractory period (RRP): Some Na+ channels recovered, but K+ channels open, increasing threshold. Stronger stimuli can elicit action potentials, but at reduced frequency. (3) Frequency encoding: Stimulus intensity encoded in firing frequency (rate coding). Stronger stimuli depolarize more, reach threshold faster, and overcome relative refractory period more quickly, producing higher frequency. (4) Adaptation: Many neurons show spike frequency adaptation—initial high frequency followed by lower steady-state frequency due to activation of slow K+ channels (e.g., Kv7, SK channels).

Clinical: Epileptic seizures involve loss of normal refractory period constraints; neurons fire at abnormally high frequencies (paroxysmal depolarizing shifts).

Significance: Refractory periods are critical for (1) ensuring unidirectional propagation (preventing back-propagation), (2) setting maximum firing rate, and (3) enabling frequency coding of stimulus intensity.

image.alt: Graph showing absolute and relative refractory periods after an action potential; paired-pulse stimulation showing reduced second response amplitude; frequency encoding: stronger stimulus produces higher firing frequency.
image.caption: Refractory Periods: Absolute refractory period (no second action potential possible) followed by relative refractory period (second action potential possible but requires stronger stimulus). Stimulus intensity is encoded in firing frequency.

classifications.value: Best for understanding how action potentials travel along axons, the structural basis of saltatory conduction, and the clinical consequences of demyelination.
characteristics: Continuous Conduction (Unmyelinated Axons): Action potential at one region depolarizes adjacent membrane via local currents (electrotonic spread). Adjacent region reaches threshold → Na+ channels open → action potential regenerated. Conduction velocity proportional to square root of axon diameter (in unmyelinated axons). Small C fibers (0.5-2 μm) conduct at 0.5-2 m/s. Energy cost high: Na+/K+-ATPase must pump ions across entire axonal membrane.

Saltatory Conduction (Myelinated Axons): Myelin sheath (Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) wraps around axon, increasing membrane resistance (Rm) and decreasing capacitance (Cm), preventing depolarization along internodes. Voltage-gated Na+ channels concentrated at nodes of Ranvier (up to 1000/μm², vs. <25/μm² in internodes). Action potential at one node generates local currents that flow through the internode (via axoplasm) and depolarize the next node. Conduction velocity proportional to axon diameter (linear relationship in myelinated axons). Large myelinated Aα fibers (13-20 μm) conduct at 80-120 m/s. Energy efficiency: Na+ entry occurs only at nodes (0.02% of membrane area in large axons), reducing Na+/K+-ATPase activity ~100-fold compared to unmyelinated.

Factors Affecting Conduction: (1) Axon diameter: larger diameter → lower axial resistance → faster conduction; (2) Temperature: cooling slows channel kinetics (Q10 2-3); (3) Demyelination: loss of myelin exposes internodal K+ channels, shunts local currents, slows or blocks conduction; (4) Channel density: reduced Na+ channel density at nodes (e.g., autoimmune attack) impairs conduction.
classifications.behavior:
Saltatory conduction is a remarkable evolutionary adaptation that enables rapid, energy-efficient signaling. The myelin sheath acts as an electrical insulator: its high resistance (R ~10⁵ Ω·cm² vs. 10³ Ω·cm² for bare membrane) and low capacitance (C ~0.01 μF/cm² vs. 1 μF/cm² for bare membrane) mean that depolarization spreads electrotonically over long distances (up to 2 mm between nodes) with minimal decay. The local current generated at an active node flows through the internodal axoplasm and depolarizes the next node; because the internodal membrane is not excitable (no Na+ channels), the current is not dissipated. The safety factor (ratio of current generated at node to current required to depolarize next node) is typically 5-10, ensuring reliable propagation.

Conduction velocity in myelinated axons follows v ∝ d (linear), while in unmyelinated axons v ∝ √d. This scaling difference explains why large myelinated axons (e.g., motor, proprioceptive) are the fastest.

Clinical correlations: Multiple sclerosis (CNS demyelination)—conduction block, slowed conduction (increased latency on evoked potentials), positive symptoms (paresthesias due to ectopic firing). Guillain-Barré syndrome (PNS demyelination)—ascending paralysis, areflexia. Charcot-Marie-Tooth type 1 (PMP22 duplication)—peripheral demyelination, slowed nerve conduction velocities. Leukodystrophies (e.g., adrenoleukodystrophy)—CNS demyelination.

Therapeutic implications: Potassium channel blockers (4-aminopyridine, dalfampridine) improve conduction in demyelinated axons by blocking exposed internodal K+ channels, prolonging depolarization and enhancing action potential propagation; FDA-approved for multiple sclerosis.

examples.explanation:
Saltatory conduction optimizes speed and efficiency: (1) Structural basis: Myelin sheaths are interrupted at nodes of Ranvier (1 μm length, spaced 0.5-2 mm apart in large axons). (2) Channel localization: Nav channels clustered at nodes (ankyrin G scaffolding); Kv channels in juxtaparanodal regions (under myelin) or at nodes in some axons. (3) Local current flow: Active node (depolarized) generates outward current that flows through extracellular fluid, enters next node (depolarizing it), and returns through axoplasm (complete circuit). (4) Energy efficiency: Na+ influx only at nodes → less Na+ to pump out → reduced ATP consumption. In human optic nerve, saltatory conduction reduces energy use ~10,000-fold compared to continuous conduction. (5) Speed: In a 1-meter-long human axon, saltatory conduction takes ~10 ms; continuous conduction would take >100 ms.

Significance: Saltatory conduction enables rapid reflexes (e.g., withdrawal reflex ~30 ms latency), precise motor control, and high-frequency sensory signaling. It is a defining feature of vertebrate nervous systems, enabling complex behaviors.

image.alt: Diagram of myelinated axon showing node of Ranvier with concentrated Na+ channels. Local current from active node flows through internode to depolarize next node. Graph shows action potentials only at nodes.
image.caption: Saltatory Conduction: Action potentials regenerate only at nodes of Ranvier. Local currents flow through the myelinated internode (high resistance, low capacitance) to depolarize the next node. This increases speed and reduces energy consumption.

examples.process: Demyelination and Conduction Block in Multiple Sclerosis
examples.explanation:
Demyelination disrupts saltatory conduction: (1) Pathophysiology: Immune-mediated destruction of myelin (CNS: multiple sclerosis; PNS: Guillain-Barré, CIDP). Myelin loss exposes internodal membrane, which contains high density of Kv1 potassium channels. (2) Conduction block: Exposed internodal K+ channels shunt local currents, preventing sufficient depolarization of the next node. This causes conduction block (failure of propagation) or slowed conduction (increased latency). (3) Clinical correlates: MS relapse—acute symptoms (weakness, numbness, visual loss) correspond to conduction block in affected pathways. Recovery may occur via remyelination (oligodendrocyte precursor cells) or redistribution of Na+ channels to internodal regions. (4) Evoked potentials: Delayed latencies (e.g., visual evoked potentials in optic neuritis) reflect demyelination. (5) Therapeutic: Dalfampridine (4-aminopyridine, K+ channel blocker) improves walking speed in MS by blocking exposed Kv channels, prolonging depolarization and restoring conduction in demyelinated axons.

Significance: Demyelinating diseases illustrate the critical role of myelin in normal conduction; understanding saltatory conduction explains both the pathophysiology and therapeutic strategies.

image.alt: Normal myelinated axon showing saltatory conduction; demyelinated axon showing current shunting through exposed internodal K+ channels, causing conduction block. Evoked potential trace showing prolonged latency in MS.
image.caption: Demyelination in MS: Myelin loss exposes internodal Kv1 channels, shunting local currents and causing conduction block. Potassium channel blockers (dalfampridine) can restore conduction by prolonging depolarization.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Action Potential Generation and Propagation

principles.principle: Electrochemical Gradients and Selective Permeability Establish the Resting Potential
principles.statement: The resting membrane potential is maintained by the Na+/K+ pump (creating concentration gradients) and selective permeability to K+ (via leak channels), resulting in a potential near the potassium equilibrium potential—a stored energy source that powers the action potential.
principles.application:
Functional inference: (1) The Na+/K+ pump consumes ~30% of neuronal ATP (up to 70% in gray matter) to maintain gradients. (2) Resting K+ permeability (K2P leak channels) sets Vm near EK (-90 mV); slight Na+ permeability shifts it to -70 mV. (3) Hyperkalemia (↑[K+]out) reduces the K+ gradient, depolarizing Vm; if Vm reaches -55 mV (threshold), Na+ channels inactivate, causing paralysis. (4) Hypokalemia (↓[K+]out) hyperpolarizes Vm, increasing excitability and causing arrhythmias.

Clinical application: Understanding resting potential explains electrolyte disorders: severe hyperkalemia (>7 mEq/L) causes cardiac arrest (inactivated Na+ channels prevent action potentials). Intravenous insulin + glucose shifts K+ into cells, acutely lowering [K+]out. Local ischemia depolarizes neurons (ATP depletion → Na+/K+ pump failure), contributing to stroke pathophysiology.
principles.example:
Hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest: (1) Normal [K+]out ~4 mEq/L, Vm ~-90 mV in cardiac myocytes. (2) Hyperkalemia (e.g., renal failure, potassium-sparing diuretics) increases [K+]out to 6-8 mEq/L. (3) Goldman equation predicts Vm depolarizes (e.g., to -70 mV at [K+]out 8 mEq/L). (4) Depolarization inactivates voltage-gated Na+ channels (reducing excitability). (5) Severe hyperkalemia (>8 mEq/L) causes progressive widening of QRS, bradycardia, asystole. (6) Emergency treatment: calcium (stabilizes membrane, restores threshold), insulin+glucose (drives K+ into cells), albuterol (β2 agonist, shifts K+), sodium bicarbonate.

Significance: The resting potential is not a fixed value but is dynamically regulated by ion gradients. Clinical management of electrolyte disorders directly applies these principles.
principles.analogy: The resting potential is like a charged battery: The Na+/K+ pump acts as the charger, maintaining the charge difference across the membrane (the battery). K+ leak channels are the "drain" that keeps the battery at a stable, negative voltage. Depolarization is like completing a circuit—the stored energy is released as an electrical pulse (action potential). If the battery is overcharged (hyperkalemia) or undercharged (hypokalemia), the system malfunctions.

image.alt: Diagram of axonal membrane showing Na+/K+ pump (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in), K+ leak channels (K+ efflux), and ion concentrations (high Na+ outside, high K+ inside). Graph shows Goldman equation prediction of Vm as function of [K+]out.
image.caption: Resting Potential: Na+/K+ pump maintains gradients; K+ leak channels create selective permeability. Vm is near EK but shifted slightly by Na+ permeability. Hyperkalemia depolarizes Vm; hypokalemia hyperpolarizes.

principles.principle: Voltage-Gated Sodium Channels Provide Regenerative Positive Feedback
principles.statement: Voltage-gated sodium channels are the engine of the action potential: depolarization opens them (activation), Na+ influx causes further depolarization (positive feedback), and rapid inactivation ensures brief, all-or-none events—the basis for threshold, all-or-none behavior, and refractory periods.
principles.application:
Channel inference: (1) Activation gate (S4 voltage sensor) opens with depolarization (milliseconds). (2) Inactivation gate (intracellular loop between domains III and IV) closes within 1-2 ms after opening. (3) The closed inactivation gate prevents reopening until repolarization allows recovery. (4) Threshold arises from cooperative activation: small depolarization opens few channels; if Na+ influx exceeds K+ efflux, net inward current accelerates, generating regenerative upstroke.

Clinical application: Local anesthetics bind to the inactivated state of Nav channels, stabilizing inactivation and raising threshold. State-dependent binding explains use-dependent block (more effective in rapidly firing neurons). Channelopathies: gain-of-function Nav mutations cause epilepsy (SCN1A, SCN2A), pain syndromes (SCN9A—erythromelalgia, paroxysmal extreme pain disorder); loss-of-function causes generalized epilepsy with febrile seizures plus (GEFS+), Dravet syndrome (SCN1A).
principles.example:
Local anesthetic mechanism: (1) Lidocaine (and other amide/ester local anesthetics) enters the Nav channel pore from the intracellular side. (2) It binds preferentially to the inactivated state (state-dependent binding). (3) Binding stabilizes inactivation, shifting the voltage dependence to more negative potentials. (4) This raises the threshold for action potential generation. (5) Use-dependent block: neurons firing at high frequency (e.g., pain fibers during surgery) have more channels in inactivated state, so are preferentially blocked. (6) Clinical: Lidocaine injected near peripheral nerves blocks pain transmission; also used as antiarrhythmic (Class IB—block cardiac Nav channels, especially in ischemic tissue).

Significance: Understanding Nav channel gating explains the mechanism of local anesthetics and antiarrhythmics, and informs structure-based drug design.
principles.analogy: Voltage-gated sodium channels are like a trapdoor with a spring-loaded latch: The voltage sensor is the latch—when it senses depolarization (someone pushes the door), it releases, the door opens (Na+ flows in). The inactivation gate is a built-in mechanism that immediately slams the door shut (even if the latch is still pressed), preventing more Na+ influx. The door cannot reopen until the latch resets (repolarization).

image.alt: Nav channel structure showing domains I-IV, S4 voltage sensor (positive charges), activation gate, inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker). Graphs show activation and inactivation curves as function of voltage; state-dependent local anesthetic binding.
image.caption: Nav Channel Gating: Depolarization moves S4 voltage sensor → activation gate opens (Na+ influx). Inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) closes rapidly, blocking pore. Local anesthetics stabilize the inactivated state, raising threshold.

principles.principle: Myelination and Node Organization Enable Saltatory Conduction
principles.statement: Myelin sheaths increase membrane resistance and decrease capacitance, restricting depolarization to nodes of Ranvier, where high-density voltage-gated sodium channels regenerate action potentials—enabling rapid, energy-efficient propagation that scales linearly with axon diameter.
principles.application:
Biophysical inference: (1) Myelin increases transverse resistance (Rm) from ~1,000 Ω·cm² to ~100,000 Ω·cm², reducing current loss through the membrane. (2) Myelin reduces capacitance (Cm) from ~1 μF/cm² to ~0.01 μF/cm², decreasing charging time constant (τ = RmCm). (3) The length constant (λ = √(Rm/Ra)) increases dramatically (from ~1 mm to >10 mm), allowing local currents to travel long distances. (4) Node spacing optimizes conduction velocity: too close reduces speed (more regenerations), too far reduces safety factor.

Clinical application: Demyelinating diseases (MS, GBS) reduce Rm and increase Cm, shortening λ, causing conduction block. Potassium channel blockers (4-AP, dalfampridine) block exposed internodal K+ channels, effectively increasing Rm and restoring conduction.
principles.example:
Dalfampridine in multiple sclerosis: (1) In demyelinated axons, internodal K+ channels (Kv1.1, Kv1.2) are exposed to extracellular space. (2) During depolarization, these K+ channels open, shunting current and preventing sufficient depolarization of the next node. (3) Dalfampridine (4-aminopyridine) is a broad-spectrum K+ channel blocker. (4) By blocking exposed Kv channels, it prolongs depolarization and enhances local current spread. (5) In MS patients, dalfampridine improves walking speed (expanded disability status scale improvement) by restoring conduction in demyelinated motor pathways. (6) Adverse effects: seizures (due to CNS hyperexcitability) at high doses.

Significance: The principle of saltatory conduction directly informs therapeutic strategies for demyelinating diseases. Understanding the biophysics of myelination explains both normal physiology and pathology.
principles.analogy: Saltatory conduction is like a relay race: Runners (action potentials) run only between exchange points (nodes). Myelin is the track that carries the baton (local current) smoothly and quickly between runners. If the track is damaged (demyelination), the baton can't be passed efficiently; the race slows (conduction block). Giving a runner a longer reach (K+ channel blocker) can help compensate.

image.alt: Equivalent circuit of myelinated axon showing high Rm and low Cm of internode, low Rm and high Cm at node. Length constant λ = √(Rm/Ra). Graph showing action potential amplitude vs. distance with and without myelin.
image.caption: Myelin Increases Length Constant: Myelinated internodes have high Rm (reduced current loss) and low Cm (faster charging), allowing local currents to travel far enough to depolarize the next node. λ increases from ~1 mm to >10 mm with myelination.

sections.section_title: Key Components of Action Potential Generation

components.definition: Voltage-gated sodium channels (Nav1.x): activation (S4 voltage sensor) and inactivation (IIII-IV linker); voltage-gated potassium channels (Kv1, Kv7, Kv11): delayed activation; Na+/K+ pump: maintains gradients; K+ leak channels (K2P): set resting potential.
components.equation: I_Na = g_Na m³ h (V - E_Na); I_K = g_K n⁴ (V - E_K); I_Na + I_K + I_L = C_m dV/dt
components.interpretation: Hodgkin-Huxley equations: m = Na activation (fast, cooperative); h = Na inactivation (slower); n = K activation (slowest). The product m³h represents the fraction of Na channels available to conduct. At rest, m≈0, h≈1. Depolarization increases m rapidly; h decays more slowly. During repolarization, m decreases rapidly, h recovers slowly—explaining refractory period. These equations reproduce action potential waveforms and have been validated across species and cell types.

image.alt: Circuit diagram of Hodgkin-Huxley model with variable Na, K, and leak conductances; graphs of m, h, n as functions of voltage; action potential simulation.
image.caption: Hodgkin-Huxley Model: I = Cm dV/dt + gNa m³h (V-ENa) + gK n⁴ (V-EK) + gL (V-EL). Gating variables m (activation), h (inactivation), n (activation) have voltage-dependent rate constants.

categories.type: Voltage-Gated Sodium Channels: Structure and Function
categories.description: Nav channels are the primary mediators of action potential upstroke; structure includes four homologous domains (each with 6 TM segments), S4 voltage sensor, and inactivation gate.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Nav channel α subunit (260 kDa): Domains I-IV (each 6 TM segments). S4 segment in each domain contains positively charged residues (arginine, lysine) at every third position—voltage sensor. Depolarization causes S4 outward movement (3-5 Å), opening activation gate (cytoplasmic S6 segments). Inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) folds into pore within 1-2 ms, blocking Na+ influx. β subunits (β1-β4) modulate channel kinetics, trafficking. Subtypes: Nav1.1 (CNS, epilepsy), Nav1.2 (CNS, developmental), Nav1.3 (embryonic, injury-induced), Nav1.4 (skeletal muscle, periodic paralysis), Nav1.5 (cardiac, arrhythmias), Nav1.6 (CNS, nodes of Ranvier), Nav1.7 (peripheral neurons, pain), Nav1.8 (DRG, TTX-resistant), Nav1.9 (DRG, TTX-resistant).

examples.process: Sodium Channelopathies: From Epilepsy to Pain
examples.explanation:
Nav channel mutations cause diverse disorders: (1) SCN1A (Nav1.1): Loss-of-function → Dravet syndrome (severe childhood epilepsy, cognitive decline, ataxia); gain-of-function → GEFS+ (milder febrile seizures). Mechanism: Nav1.1 expressed primarily in GABAergic inhibitory interneurons; loss-of-function reduces inhibition → hyperexcitability. (2) SCN9A (Nav1.7): Gain-of-function → inherited erythromelalgia (burning pain, redness); loss-of-function → congenital insensitivity to pain (no pain perception, often self-injury). (3) SCN4A (Nav1.4): Gain-of-function → paramyotonia congenita (cold-induced muscle stiffness); loss-of-function → hypokalemic periodic paralysis (episodic weakness with low K+). (4) SCN5A (Nav1.5): Gain-of-function → long QT syndrome type 3 (delayed repolarization, arrhythmia risk); loss-of-function → Brugada syndrome (ST elevation, sudden death).

Significance: Channelopathies illustrate the critical role of Nav channels in excitability; they provide natural experiments linking molecular structure to clinical phenotype and guide precision medicine approaches (e.g., mexiletine for Nav1.4 gain-of-function).

image.alt: Diagram of Nav channel with mutation sites; clinical phenotypes: Dravet syndrome (SCN1A), erythromelalgia (SCN9A), paramyotonia (SCN4A), long QT3 (SCN5A).
image.caption: Nav Channelopathies: Mutations in specific Nav isoforms cause distinct disorders. SCN1A (CNS inhibitory interneurons) → epilepsy; SCN9A (pain fibers) → erythromelalgia or pain insensitivity; SCN4A (muscle) → periodic paralysis; SCN5A (heart) → cardiac arrhythmias.

categories.type: Voltage-Gated Potassium Channels: Repolarization and Excitability Control
categories.description: Kv channels mediate repolarization, set firing frequency, and regulate neurotransmitter release; diverse family with distinct gating properties and physiological roles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Kv channel structure: tetramer of α subunits (each 6 TM segments, S4 voltage sensor). Activation slower than Na channels (τ ~1-5 ms). Subfamilies: Kv1 (Shaker-related): delayed rectifiers, including Kv1.1 (juxtaparanodal, epilepsy), Kv1.4 (A-type, fast inactivation); Kv4 (Shal-related): A-type (rapid activation/inactivation, somatodendritic, shaping EPSPs); Kv7 (KCNQ): M-current (slow, non-inactivating, regulates excitability; Kv7.2/7.3 mutations cause epilepsy); Kv11 (hERG): cardiac repolarization (Kv11.1 mutations cause long QT syndrome type 2).

examples.explanation:
Kv7 channels (KCNQ2, KCNQ3) generate the M-current—a slow, voltage-dependent, non-inactivating K+ current that activates near threshold and opposes repetitive firing. (1) M-current activation: depolarization from rest opens Kv7 channels; the resulting K+ efflux opposes further depolarization. (2) Function: M-current acts as a 'brake' on neuronal excitability, preventing excessive firing. (3) Modulation: M-current is inhibited by muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M1) via PLC/PIP2 depletion; this increases excitability (e.g., in hippocampus during learning). (4) Mutations: KCNQ2/KCNQ3 loss-of-function cause benign familial neonatal epilepsy (BFNE)—reduced M-current → neuronal hyperexcitability → seizures in newborns. (5) Pharmacology: Retigabine (ezogabine) is a Kv7 opener (anti-epileptic; withdrawn due to side effects).

Significance: Kv7 channels illustrate the role of voltage-gated K+ channels in excitability control and are critical therapeutic targets in epilepsy, pain, and neuropsychiatric disorders.

image.alt: Diagram of Kv7 channel (KCNQ2/3) with modulators; graph showing M-current activation near threshold; effect of M-current on firing frequency; mutation in BFNE.
image.caption: Kv7 M-Current: Slow, non-inactivating K+ current activated near threshold, opposing repetitive firing. KCNQ2/3 mutations reduce M-current, causing neonatal epilepsy. M-current is inhibited by M1 muscarinic receptors, linking excitability to neuromodulation.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Analyzing Action Potential Disorders

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Action Potential Disorders
worked_examples.problem: A 35-year-old woman presents with episodes of muscle stiffness triggered by cold, followed by weakness. She reports that after sitting in air conditioning, her hand muscles become stiff (cannot release grip), then after 10-15 minutes become weak. Family history: mother has similar episodes. Laboratory: normal electrolytes. Based on action potential physiology: (1) What is the likely diagnosis? (2) Which ion channel is affected? (3) What is the mechanism of cold-induced stiffness and subsequent weakness? (4) What treatment would be appropriate?

step_by_step.calculation: Cold-induced muscle stiffness (myotonia) followed by weakness (paralysis). Autosomal dominant inheritance. Normal electrolytes (ruling out metabolic myopathies). Pattern suggests a skeletal muscle channelopathy affecting voltage-gated sodium channels. Myotonia (delayed relaxation after contraction) indicates repetitive firing of muscle action potentials. Cold sensitivity is characteristic of paramyotonia congenita.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the affected channel and mutation type.
step_by_step.calculation: Paramyotonia congenita is caused by gain-of-function mutations in SCN4A (Nav1.4, skeletal muscle sodium channel). Mutations (e.g., R1448H, T1313M) impair fast inactivation—the inactivation gate fails to close properly. This causes persistent Na+ current during depolarization, leading to prolonged depolarization and repetitive firing (myotonia). Cold exacerbates by further slowing inactivation kinetics.

step_by_step.explanation: Explain the biphasic response (stiffness then weakness).
step_by_step.calculation: Cold-induced stiffness (myotonia): Impaired inactivation causes persistent Na+ current → prolonged depolarization → sustained muscle contraction (stiffness). Cold slows inactivation further, worsening stiffness. Subsequent weakness (paralysis): Prolonged depolarization (from persistent Na+ current) eventually inactivates Na+ channels (use-dependent inactivation). Once channels are inactivated, no action potentials can be generated → flaccid weakness. Warmth allows recovery of inactivation. This explains episodic weakness after cold exposure.

Treatment targets the underlying channel dysfunction: (1) Mexiletine (Class IB antiarrhythmic, Nav blocker) reduces persistent Na+ current by stabilizing the inactivated state; reduces myotonia and weakness. (2) Avoid cold exposure. (3) Avoid depolarizing agents (succinylcholine) and potassium shifts that worsen symptoms. (4) For hyperkalemic periodic paralysis (different channelopathy, also SCN4A), acetazolamide may be used.

Significance: This case illustrates how understanding Nav channel gating (activation, inactivation) explains clinical phenomena (cold sensitivity, myotonia, paralysis) and guides targeted therapy.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Likely diagnosis: Paramyotonia congenita (a skeletal muscle sodium channelopathy). (2) Affected channel: SCN4A (Nav1.4, voltage-gated sodium channel) with gain-of-function mutation impairing fast inactivation. (3) Mechanism: Cold slows already impaired inactivation → persistent Na+ current → prolonged depolarization → myotonia (stiffness). Prolonged depolarization inactivates Na+ channels → flaccid weakness. (4) Treatment: Mexiletine (Nav blocker) reduces persistent Na+ current; avoid cold exposure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Action potential physiology—specifically Nav channel gating, inactivation, and temperature sensitivity—explains the clinical features of paramyotonia congenita. The biphasic response (stiffness then weakness) reflects the interplay between persistent Na+ current (myotonia) and use-dependent inactivation (paralysis). This understanding enables targeted pharmacotherapy (mexiletine) and patient education.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Action Potential Generation and Propagation
sections.introduction: The action potential is generated by sequential voltage-gated ion channel opening and propagates via local currents. This flow integrates membrane biophysics with axon structure to produce rapid, reliable signaling.

process_flow.description: Neuron at rest with stable membrane potential, ion gradients maintained by Na+/K+ pump, and voltage-gated channels closed.
process_flow.mechanism: Na+/K+ pump maintains high [K+]in, low [Na+]in. K+ leak channels (K2P) keep membrane permeable to K+, setting Vm near EK (-90 mV). Small Na+ permeability shifts Vm to -70 mV. Voltage-gated Na+ channels are closed (activation gate closed, inactivation gate open). Voltage-gated K+ channels are closed.
process_flow.result: Stable resting potential, ready for stimulation

image.alt: Axonal membrane at rest: Na+/K+ pump, K+ leak channels open, Nav channel (activation gate closed, inactivation gate open), Kv channel closed. Vm = -70 mV.
image.caption: Step 1: Resting State. K+ leak channels maintain resting potential near EK. Nav channels are closed but available (inactivation gate open).

process_flow.description: Excitatory input (EPSP) depolarizes the membrane to threshold (~-55 mV), triggering positive feedback.
process_flow.mechanism: Synaptic input (or local current from adjacent active node) depolarizes membrane. Small depolarization opens a few Nav channels (activation gate opens). Na+ influx causes further depolarization, opening more Nav channels (positive feedback). Once threshold is reached, the process becomes regenerative.
process_flow.result: Membrane potential reaches threshold; rapid depolarization begins

image.alt: Depolarization to threshold: a few Nav channels open, Na+ influx causes further depolarization. Graph shows membrane potential crossing threshold.
image.caption: Step 2: Depolarization to Threshold. Initial depolarization opens a few Nav channels; Na+ influx causes further depolarization, creating positive feedback. Once threshold is reached, full action potential is triggered.

process_flow.description: Rapid, regenerative Na+ influx drives membrane potential toward ENa, producing action potential peak.
process_flow.mechanism: All Nav channels open (activation gates open). Na+ influx (gNa increases from <1 to >100 mS/cm²) drives Vm toward ENa (+60 mV). Overshoot reaches +30 to +50 mV. K+ channels remain closed at this stage.
process_flow.result: Membrane potential peaks at +30 to +50 mV

image.alt: Upstroke: all Nav channels open, Na+ influx drives Vm to +30 mV. Kv channels still closed.
image.caption: Step 3: Depolarization (Upstroke). Voltage-gated Na+ channels fully open; massive Na+ influx drives membrane potential toward ENa, producing the action potential peak.

process_flow.description: Na+ channels inactivate; K+ channels open, allowing K+ efflux to restore negative potential.
process_flow.mechanism: Inactivation gate of Nav channels closes (blocks pore, ~1-2 ms after opening). Delayed rectifier Kv channels open (n⁴ gating). K+ efflux (down electrochemical gradient) repolarizes membrane. Na+ channels remain inactivated until repolarization occurs.
process_flow.result: Membrane potential returns toward resting level

image.alt: Repolarization: Nav channels inactivated (h gate closed), Kv channels open, K+ efflux repolarizes Vm.
image.caption: Step 4: Repolarization. Nav channels inactivate (inactivation gate closes), blocking further Na+ influx. Kv channels open; K+ efflux repolarizes the membrane.

process_flow.description: Slow closure of K+ channels causes transient hyperpolarization; Na+ channels recover from inactivation.
process_flow.mechanism: Kv channels close slowly, allowing continued K+ efflux below resting potential (afterhyperpolarization, AHP). During this period, Na+ channels recover from inactivation (h gate reopens). Relative refractory period: stronger stimulus required to reach threshold due to residual K+ conductance.
process_flow.result: Membrane returns to resting potential; channels ready for next action potential

image.alt: Afterhyperpolarization: Vm dips below resting potential due to slow K+ channel closure. Na+ channels recover from inactivation (h gate reopens).
image.caption: Step 5: Afterhyperpolarization and Recovery. Slow Kv channel closure causes AHP. During this period, Na+ channels recover from inactivation, restoring availability for the next action potential.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Molecular Structure of Voltage-Gated Ion Channels
sections.definition: Voltage-gated ion channels are macromolecular complexes with voltage-sensing domains (S4 segments), pore-forming regions (selectivity filter, activation gate), and inactivation mechanisms (ball-and-chain, hinged-lid) that enable rapid, voltage-dependent gating.

process_types.type: Nav Channel Structure: Voltage Sensing and Inactivation
process_types.energy_profile: Nav channels convert electrical energy (membrane potential) into mechanical movement (S4 translation) to open the activation gate; inactivation involves a hinged-lid mechanism (IIII-IV linker).
process_types.direction: Resting (closed): S4 segments are "down," activation gate closed, inactivation gate open. Depolarization: S4 moves outward (translation 3-5 Å, rotation), pulling S6 segments to open activation gate. Channel opens (conducting). Inactivation: III-IV linker (inactivation gate) folds into pore, blocking Na+ flux. Repolarization: S4 returns, activation gate closes; inactivation gate retreats, returning to resting state.
process_types.function: Rapid, voltage-dependent Na+ influx for action potential upstroke; inactivation ensures brief depolarization
goals: Understand molecular basis of voltage sensing

primary_example.process: Cryo-EM Structure of Nav1.7 and Disease Mutations
primary_example.explanation:
Recent cryo-EM structures of Nav1.7 reveal: (1) Four domains arranged pseudosymmetrically around central pore. (2) S4 voltage sensors in each domain with gating charges (Arg/Lys residues). (3) Inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) sits near the pore, stabilized by interactions with III S4-S5 linker. (4) Disease mutations map to functional regions: R1448H (paramyotonia) in IV S4 disrupts inactivation; F1449V (epilepsy) alters voltage dependence; I848T (erythromelalgia) in II S5 enhances channel activation. (5) Local anesthetic binding site: within the pore, between IV S6 and domain I, accessed from intracellular side (state-dependent).

Significance: High-resolution structures reveal the atomic basis of voltage gating, inactivation, and pharmacology. They enable structure-based drug design for channelopathies and pain.

image.alt: Cryo-EM structure of Nav1.7 showing four domains, S4 voltage sensors, inactivation gate, pore. Inset shows mutation sites mapped to structure.
image.caption: Nav1.7 Cryo-EM Structure: Four domains (I-IV) surround central pore. S4 voltage sensors contain gating charges. Inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) blocks pore. Channelopathy mutations map to voltage sensors, pore, and inactivation gate.

image.alt: Schematic of Nav channel α subunit showing domains I-IV, S4 segments with positive charges, activation gate (S6), inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker).
image.caption: Nav Channel Structure: Four homologous domains (I-IV), each with 6 TM segments. S4 (voltage sensor) contains positively charged residues. Inactivation gate (IIII-IV linker) folds into pore to block Na+ influx.

process_types.type: Kv Channel Structure: Delayed Rectifier and Inactivation
process_types.energy_profile: Kv channels are tetramers with voltage-sensing domains (S4) that activate slowly, enabling delayed repolarization; some subtypes have N-type inactivation (ball-and-chain).
process_types.direction: Resting (closed): S4 "down," pore closed. Depolarization: S4 moves outward, opens activation gate (S6 bundle crossing). Channel opens (K+ efflux). N-type inactivation: N-terminal 'ball' domain occludes pore from intracellular side (ball-and-chain). C-type inactivation: conformational change at selectivity filter.
process_types.function: Repolarization after action potential; regulate firing frequency; shape excitability
goals: Correlate Kv channel mutations with disorders

primary_example.explanation:
Kv7 channels (KCNQ2, KCNQ3) are voltage-gated K+ channels that generate M-current. (1) Structure: Kv7 subunits form tetramers; each has 6 TM segments, S4 voltage sensor, and a long C-terminus that binds calmodulin (CaM) and PIP2. (2) Activation: Kv7 channels activate at subthreshold voltages (-60 to -40 mV), slowly (τ ~50-100 ms), and do not inactivate. (3) M-current function: Provides a 'brake' on repetitive firing; loss reduces threshold and increases firing. (4) Mutations: KCNQ2/3 loss-of-function cause benign familial neonatal epilepsy (BFNE) and more severe developmental epileptic encephalopathies. (5) Modulation: M-current is inhibited by Gq-coupled receptors (M1 muscarinic, bradykinin) via PIP2 depletion, linking neuromodulation to excitability.

Significance: Kv7 channels illustrate the role of voltage-gated K+ channels in excitability control and are important therapeutic targets (retigabine, now withdrawn; new Kv7 openers in development).

image.alt: Kv7 channel structure showing tetramer, voltage sensors, C-terminal calmodulin binding; graph of M-current activation; effect of mutation on firing.
image.caption: Kv7 (KCNQ) Channels: Tetrameric structure with S4 voltage sensors. M-current activates near threshold, opposing depolarization. KCNQ2/3 loss-of-function mutations reduce M-current, causing neuronal hyperexcitability and epilepsy.

image.alt: Kv channel structure showing tetramer of α subunits, pore, S4 voltage sensor; N-type inactivation ball-and-chain; C-type inactivation.
image.caption: Kv Channel Structure: Tetramer of α subunits (each 6 TM). Voltage sensing (S4) opens activation gate. N-type inactivation: N-terminal 'ball' occludes pore. C-type inactivation involves selectivity filter collapse.

sections.relationship: The molecular structures of voltage-gated sodium and potassium channels provide the physical basis for action potential generation. Voltage sensors (S4 segments) convert changes in membrane potential into conformational changes; activation gates control ion flux; inactivation mechanisms terminate the response. Channelopathies—mutations that disrupt these structures—cause disorders of excitability (epilepsy, periodic paralysis, cardiac arrhythmias, pain syndromes). Understanding structure-function relationships enables rational drug design and precision medicine.

summary:
The action potential is the fundamental electrical signal of excitable cells, generated by the coordinated activity of voltage-gated ion channels.

The resting membrane potential (-65 to -70 mV) is established by the Na+/K+ pump (maintaining ion gradients) and K+ leak channels (selective permeability), placing the membrane near EK (-90 mV).

Depolarization to threshold (~-55 mV) activates voltage-gated sodium channels (Nav), whose S4 voltage sensors trigger channel opening; Na+ influx drives rapid depolarization toward ENa (+60 mV) via regenerative positive feedback.

Repolarization occurs through Nav inactivation (IIII-IV linker occludes pore) and delayed opening of voltage-gated potassium channels (Kv), allowing K+ efflux.

The refractory periods—absolute (Nav inactivated, no response) and relative (Kv open, higher threshold)—ensure unidirectional propagation and limit firing frequency.

Propagation occurs via local currents: depolarization at one site spreads electrotonically to depolarize adjacent membrane.

In myelinated axons, myelin (high resistance, low capacitance) restricts depolarization to nodes of Ranvier, where Nav channels are concentrated; action potentials jump between nodes—saltatory conduction—increasing velocity (up to 120 m/s) and reducing energy consumption (~100-fold vs. unmyelinated).

Clinical correlations: Channelopathies—Nav mutations cause epilepsy (SCN1A), periodic paralysis (SCN4A), pain syndromes (SCN9A), cardiac arrhythmias (SCN5A); Kv7 mutations cause neonatal epilepsy (KCNQ2/3). Demyelinating diseases (multiple sclerosis, Guillain-Barré) disrupt saltatory conduction, causing conduction block and neurological deficits; potassium channel blockers (dalfampridine) can restore conduction. Local anesthetics (lidocaine) block Nav channels in the inactivated state, preventing action potentials.

Understanding action potential physiology—from Hodgkin-Huxley biophysics to molecular channel structure—is foundational for neurology, cardiology, anesthesiology, and pharmacology.
